challenges.title: Home Water Audit
challenges.description: Check every faucet, toilet, and showerhead at home for leaks. A single dripping faucet can waste 3,000 gallons a year! Document what you find and estimate the water savings if fixed.
challenges.shortDescription: Find and report water leaks at home
steps: Walk through your home and check each faucet for drips
Listen for running water in each toilet (put food coloring in the tank — if color appears in the bowl within 15 minutes, you have a leak)
Check under sinks for any moisture or slow drips
Take a photo of any leaks you find
Write a short reflection on what you discovered and how much water could be saved
tips: A dripping faucet can waste up to 3,000 gallons per year!
Put food coloring in toilet tanks — if color appears in the bowl, you have a leak
Check your water meter before and after a 2-hour no-use period to spot hidden leaks
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Cerritos saves 722 million gallons of potable water per year through its recycled water program — your leak-finding helps protect this resource further.
challenges.financialSnippet: The city's Water Enterprise Fund maintains 25 miles of recycled water lines serving 200+ acres. Every leak fixed at home means less stress on the water system.

challenges.title: Recycled Water Trail Walk
challenges.description: Walk through your neighborhood and identify parks, medians, and school grounds irrigated with recycled water (look for purple pipe markers or signs). Map the locations and learn how Cerritos saves 722 million gallons of drinking water every year.
challenges.shortDescription: Map recycled water sites in your neighborhood
steps: Plan a walking route through parks and along major medians in your neighborhood
Look for purple pipe markers, signs, or valve covers indicating recycled water use
Take photos of each recycled water marker you find
Note the location and what's being irrigated (park, median, school, golf course)
Write a reflection: How much water do you think is saved at each site? Why does this matter?
tips: Purple pipes and markers = recycled water! It's the universal color code
Heritage Park, Liberty Park, and the Iron-Wood Nine golf course are good starting points
Cerritos has had recycled water since 1988 — nearly 4 decades!
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Cerritos's recycled water program has operated since 1988, conserving 722 million gallons of potable water annually across 200+ irrigated acres.
challenges.financialSnippet: The recycled water system uses 25 miles of distribution lines. Every acre irrigated with recycled water saves the city money on increasingly expensive potable water.

challenges.title: Park Cleanup Champion
challenges.description: Visit one of Cerritos's 23+ parks and spend 30 minutes picking up litter. Bring a bag and gloves, document what you collect, and reflect on the park's condition.
challenges.shortDescription: Clean up litter at a Cerritos park
steps: Choose one of Cerritos's 23+ parks to visit
Bring a trash bag and gloves (or use a repurposed bag)
Spend at least 30 minutes picking up litter — focus on areas around benches, playgrounds, and walkways
Take a before-and-after photo of the area you cleaned
Write a short reflection: What types of litter did you find? What could prevent it?
tips: Heritage Park, Liberty Park, and Frontier Park are great starting points
Avoid picking up anything sharp or hazardous — leave that for professionals
Bring a friend to make it more fun and cover more ground
challenges.cityGoalConnection: The city's $4.5M Parks & Trees Management budget maintains 23+ parks and 28,000+ trees. Community cleanups extend these resources.
challenges.financialSnippet: Parks & Trees Management costs $4.5M/year for FY 2025-26. That's roughly $195K per park. Every volunteer hour saves taxpayer money for other improvements.

challenges.title: Tree Care Ambassador
challenges.description: Adopt a street tree or neighborhood tree for a month. Water it weekly, check for damage, and document its health. Cerritos maintains 28,000+ trees and has been Tree City USA for 25+ consecutive years!
challenges.shortDescription: Adopt and care for a neighborhood tree
steps: Identify a street tree or young tree in your neighborhood that needs attention
Take a 'before' photo showing its current condition
Water it deeply once a week for 4 weeks (young trees need 15-20 gallons per watering)
Check for signs of damage, pests, or disease each time you visit
After 4 weeks, take an 'after' photo and write about what you learned about urban trees
tips: Water slowly at the base — a slow trickle from a hose for 15-20 minutes works great
Young trees (planted in the last 3 years) need the most help during hot months
If you notice serious damage, report it to the city's Public Works department
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Cerritos has been Tree City USA for 25+ consecutive years, maintaining 28,000+ public trees. Community tree stewards multiply the city's capacity.
challenges.financialSnippet: The Parks & Trees Management budget is $4.5M for FY 2025-26. About 250 new trees are planted each year. Each healthy tree adds ~$20 in annual benefits through air cleaning and cooling.

challenges.title: Solar Savings Detective
challenges.description: Investigate Cerritos's solar energy story — from the nation's first solar-heated City Hall in 1978 to today's 350,000 kWh Corporate Yard solar farm. Calculate the energy and cost savings.
challenges.shortDescription: Investigate the city's solar energy savings
steps: Research the history: Cerritos City Hall installed 1,408 sq ft of solar panels in 1978
Find out how much the Corporate Yard solar array generates (350,000 kWh/year)
Calculate the savings: at $0.20/kWh, how much does the solar farm save annually?
Compare: how many homes could 350,000 kWh power for a year? (avg CA home uses ~6,500 kWh)
Write a reflection: Should Cerritos expand solar? Where would you put new panels?
tips: 350,000 kWh × $0.20/kWh = $70,000/year in electricity savings!
That's enough to power about 54 average California homes for a year
The 1978 City Hall solar system was one of the very first in the nation for a government building
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Cerritos has been a solar energy pioneer since 1978. The Facility Maintenance budget includes operations for the city's solar and energy infrastructure.
challenges.financialSnippet: Facility maintenance costs $3.0M/year (FY 2025-26). The Corporate Yard solar farm saves ~$70K/year in electricity costs, and LED traffic signals save 60% energy citywide.

challenges.title: Auto Square Economics Explorer
challenges.description: Discover how the Cerritos Auto Square — one of the world's largest auto malls — funds your city services. Calculate how buying a car locally generates tax revenue that pays for parks, safety, and more.
challenges.shortDescription: Learn how local shopping funds city services
steps: Look up: Cerritos sales tax revenue is $43.8M — the Auto Square generates about $14.6M of that
Calculate: If someone buys a $40,000 car, roughly $400 comes back to the city as sales tax
Figure out what $400 buys: Use the Tax Dollar Calculator to see where it goes
Research: What percentage of the city budget comes from sales tax? (hint: 43.2%!)
Write a reflection: Why does it matter where you shop? What happens if an auto dealer closes?
tips: The Auto Square has been the backbone of Cerritos finance since the 1980s
Sales tax from the Auto Square alone pays for more than the entire Community Development budget
If sales tax drops $1M, the city has to find cuts — that's why auto sales headwinds matter
challenges.cityGoalConnection: The city's new Economic Development team works to retain businesses and attract new ones, keeping revenue strong. Economic sustainability IS environmental sustainability.
challenges.financialSnippet: Sales Tax: $43.8M (43.2% of General Fund revenue). Auto Square: ~$14.6M. When FY 2024-25 auto sales slowed, the city adjusted its forecast down from $46M to $42.8M.

challenges.title: Recycling Contamination Audit
challenges.description: Inspect your household's recycling bin before pickup day. Identify items that DON'T belong (contamination) and create a family guide for what's actually recyclable in Cerritos.
challenges.shortDescription: Audit your recycling bin for contamination
steps: On the day before recycling pickup, carefully inspect your blue bin's contents
Sort items into 'correctly recycled' and 'contamination' piles
Take a photo showing the contamination items you found
Research Cerritos's recycling guidelines from Athens Services to understand what's accepted
Create a simple one-page recycling guide for your family (can be handwritten or digital)
Write a reflection: What was the most surprising contaminant?
tips: Common contaminants: plastic bags, food-soiled containers, styrofoam, and 'wishful recycling'
Greasy pizza boxes go in the green waste, not recycling!
All Athens Services waste vehicles in Cerritos run on CNG — cleaner fuel for cleaner streets
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Athens Services handles all waste collection in Cerritos with CNG-powered trucks. Reducing contamination makes recycling more efficient and cost-effective.
challenges.financialSnippet: Public Works spends $3.0M/year on vehicle maintenance including the CNG fleet. Cleaner recycling means fewer processing costs and better diversion rates.

challenges.title: Budget Detective Challenge
challenges.description: Dive into the real Cerritos FY 2025-26 budget and find answers to key questions. How much does the city spend per resident? What's the biggest department? Where does the money come from?
steps: Use the Budget Explorer to find: What is the total General Fund operating budget?
Calculate the per-capita spending: Total ÷ 51,460 residents
Identify the top 3 departments by spending and their percentages
Find 3 sustainability-related line items and their budgets
Discover: What percentage of revenue comes from sales tax?
Write a 'Budget Brief' — a 1-paragraph summary as if explaining to a friend
tips: General Fund operating expenditures: $91.5M. All-Funds: $131.4M
Community Safety is the #1 department — sheriff and fire contracts together
Sales tax is 43.2% of General Fund revenue — nearly half!
challenges.cityGoalConnection: Cerritos's Finance Department has won the GFOA Distinguished Budget Presentation Award. Understanding the budget is the first step to civic engagement.
challenges.financialSnippet: Administrative Services spends $11.4M/year, including the Finance & Budget team that produces the 370-page budget document. They manage $131.4M in total city spending.
